and he was pronouncing it as Axe-Hole. Axe-Hole? No, bad name, bad. And he totally ruined the name. He ruined the name. I can't name my son that name now. They weren't able to stop laughing. They loved it. And I love this particular story as well. And I just want you to remember that it takes time and experience to be able to tell stories in a shorter format. Don't hold yourself to that level of expectation. It takes time to develop this skill. While you're writing and documenting your stories, a quick tip here, don't try to edit them as you're writing. The writing process and the edit process are two different processes. So make sure while you're writing the story, just write, don't try to shorten it. I want to share with you a few extra tips that you can use to help you shorten your stories. The first one, cut out unnecessary adverbs. These are the L-Y words. You know, replace them with the strong words. For example, instead of saying, we are fairly confident that our product will most likely help you. Change this to, we are confident that our product will help you. Notice how I got rid of the L-Y words? The second thing you can do, reduce the number of backstories. They may not be necessary. Try telling them without as much context. Three, reduce the number of characters in your story. Four, reduce the character description. Don't take too long to describe the characters. Five, reduce the scope or timeframe of your story. Instead of talking about something that happened over three years, share the moment of transformation, the most powerful moment. Keep these five tips in mind because they're going to help you begin the process of being able to shorten your stories. Here's a really cool psychological tip as well. If you're still feeling a little bit antsy about sharing stories, there's a really cool sentence you can use right at the beginning of your storytelling process. And it calms you down and it relaxes the audience as well. You say the words before we start. Because when you say the words before we start or before I start, it tricks your brain into thinking, well, you haven't started yet, so it's all good. Of course you've got time to share a story. And then also it disarms the audience too because the audience is like, oh, it's okay for him to share a story. He hasn't started yet.
